title: Central Banks Tighten Across the Board as Oil Glut Looms and Trade Wars Multiply
subhead: The BOJ signals further rate hikes, the Fed's dot plot flips hawkish, and the ECB holds firm at 2.25% — all while oil markets face a whiplash from Hormuz recovery and G7 fractures deepen over tariffs and China.

sections.heading: BOJ Summary Affirms Rate Hike Stance as Inflation Risks Mount
sections.body:
A summary of opinions from the Bank of Japan's June 19 policy meeting, released on June 24, shows that some board members called for further interest rate hikes to push the policy rate closer to levels deemed neutral for the economy. The BOJ raised its benchmark rate at the meeting — the third hike in this cycle — to the highest level since 1995, according to Bloomberg.

The summary confirms the BOJ's growing confidence that wage-driven inflation is becoming embedded. Board members flagged upside risks to prices from the weak yen and persistent energy costs, even as they acknowledged uncertainty from US tariff policy and the global growth outlook. The yen had already been under pressure from the rate differential with the US, and the Fed's hawkish tilt this week only widens that gap.

Former BOJ board member Seiji Adachi had told Bloomberg in late May that a June hike was 'probable,' and the summary validates that the internal debate has moved from whether to hike to how fast. The BOJ is no longer an outlier in global tightening — it is now aligned with a broadening inflation fight across developed-market central banks.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan is exiting decades of ultra-loose policy at precisely the moment when global rate expectations are shifting hawkish. The BOJ's pace of normalization will directly affect yen carry trades, JGB yields, and the cost of funding for one of the world's largest sovereign debt markets.

sections.heading: Fed's Warsh Era Begins with Hawkish Dot Plot, No Forward Guidance
sections.body:
Kevin Warsh's first meeting as Fed chair delivered a clear message: nine of 18 FOMC participants now expect rates to be higher by year-end, with six of those supporting two or more quarter-point increases, according to AP News. Just three months ago, no policymaker had penciled in a hike.

The Fed kept its key rate unchanged at 3.50%-3.75%, but the statement dropped all forward guidance language suggesting the next move would be a cut. Warsh told reporters that guidance is 'not well suited to the current policy conjuncture,' signaling a deliberate shift away from the Powell-era practice of telegraphing rate paths. He also declined to submit his own dot-plot projection and announced a review of Fed communications strategy.

The inflation picture is driving the shift: the May CPI came in at 4.2% year-over-year, and the April PCE at 3.8% — both far above the 2% target. Officials now project 2026 inflation at 3.6%, up from 2.7% in March. Warsh was blunt: 'We've missed on inflation for five years and we're going to fix that.' Deutsche Bank's chief US economist Matthew Luzzetti said the risk of rate hikes 'has clearly risen given what we got today.' UBS pushed back its rate cut forecast to March 2027. Markets are pricing a 66% probability of at least one hike by October, according to BeInCrypto.

Warsh also announced the creation of task forces to review major Fed operations, and sidestepped questions about his contact with Trump, saying only that his weekly breakfasts with Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent are a 'long-running tradition.'
sections.whyItMatters: The Fed's shift from an easing bias to a hiking bias — in a single quarter — changes the trajectory for every asset class. Warsh's rejection of forward guidance means markets will price Fed policy entirely from data releases, increasing volatility. The political angle matters too: Trump says he trusts Warsh to be 'totally independent,' which paradoxically gives the new chair more room to hike than Powell ever had.

sections.heading: ECB's Lane: Euro Zone Inflation May Stay Above 2% Even With Mideast Peace
sections.body:
ECB Chief Economist Philip Lane said on June 23 that euro zone inflation could remain above the ECB's 2% target for some time, even if the Middle East peace deal holds and energy prices ease. His comments come a week after the ECB raised rates by 25 basis points to 2.25% — its first hike since September 2023, according to CryptoBriefing and Seeking Alpha.

Lane characterized the response as 'measured,' suggesting the ECB does not currently plan an aggressive tightening cycle. But his explicit acknowledgment that inflation may stay elevated even after the Iran war premium fades signals that the ECB sees underlying price pressure beyond energy — particularly in services and food. Euro zone flash PMIs, due this week, will provide the next read on whether the rate hike is biting.

The timing is significant: the ECB is tightening while the euro zone economy is already feeling the drag from elevated energy costs and Chinese import competition. Macron told the G7 that Chinese exports are 'literally killing a large part of European industry,' and the ECB must now weigh inflation against an industrial sector already under pressure from trade diversion.
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB is the last major central bank to begin tightening in this cycle, starting from a much lower rate floor. If inflation proves stickier than Lane's 'measured' outlook assumes, the ECB will face a more aggressive catch-up — with direct implications for Italian and Greek sovereign spreads.

sections.heading: Oil Whiplash: IEA Sees 2027 Glut Even as Inventories Hit Historic Lows
sections.body:
The IEA's June monthly oil market report lays out a dramatic reversal story. Global oil supply slumped to 94.5 million barrels per day in May, down 600,000 b/d month-on-month, with observed inventories plunging by 143 million barrels in May alone after a 74 million barrel draw in April. Inventories have shed about 3.8 million barrels per day since the Iran war began on February 28.

But the forward picture flips entirely. The IEA now expects supply to surge by roughly 8 million b/d to around 110 mb/d in 2027, massively outstripping a modest demand recovery of 2 million b/d to 105.3 mb/d. 'Our first look at 2027 balances shows a significant overhang emerging next year,' the IEA wrote.

Brent crude traded at $78.44 and WTI at $75.18 on June 17, both near three-month lows, as markets priced in the US-Iran ceasefire deal set to be signed in Geneva on Friday. But the physical market tells a different story: roughly 500 commercial vessels remain trapped in the Persian Gulf, according to Kpler, and clearing mines from shipping lanes could take six months. Kpler's Amena Bakr estimated that restarting production to prewar levels would take an additional three months beyond that.

The AP reports that Iran has demanded the right to collect tolls from ships using the Strait, while Trump claimed the deal involves 'toll free opening' — a discrepancy that could yet complicate the recovery. Lloyd's List warned that the shipping sector is 'not rushing back' until mine clearance and safe transit lanes are confirmed.
sections.whyItMatters: The oil market is pricing a smooth Hormuz reopening while the physical market is operating on fumes. The 2027 glut forecast is already pulling forward long-dated crude lower, but near-term inventory draws could push spot prices higher if the Hormuz clearance drags. For central banks, this creates a worst-of-both-worlds: current inflation pressure from depleted stocks, followed by a deflationary glut next year.

sections.heading: G7 Trade Fractures: Trump's 100% French Wine Tariff, China Shock 2.0, and Critical Minerals
sections.body:
The G7 summit in Evian was supposed to present a united front. Instead it exposed deepening trade fissures on three fronts.

Tariffs on allies: Trump threatened a 100% tariff on French wine and champagne unless Paris removes its 3% digital services tax on US tech companies. France24 reports that Trump told the New York Post he 'asked Macron not to charge American companies,' and that if France refuses, he would 'have no choice.' French wine and spirits exports to the US already face a 15% tariff and fell 21% last year, per the French Federation of Wine and Spirits Exporters. Macron responded that France 'would not bow to pressure' and scrap the tax, Reuters reported. Canada already scrapped its own digital services tax under US pressure last year.

China Shock 2.0: AP reports that China recorded a record $1.2 trillion global trade surplus last year, with exports now redirecting away from US tariff walls toward Europe and Asia. Macron told the G7 that Chinese exports are 'literally killing a large part of European industry.' Peterson Institute senior fellow Maurice Obstfeld warned that 'China's export surge, unless its leaders rein it in, will provoke a protectionist wave against Chinese imports worldwide.' The EU currently imposes relatively low baseline tariffs on China, with targeted duties of up to 35% on electric vehicles, but the G7 discussed aligning a collective response.

Critical minerals: Japan's PM Takaichi proposed a strategic mineral stockpiling plan at the G7, Reuters reported, as China throttles shipments of key minerals to Japan. Bloomberg reported on June 23 that Xi is pressuring Takaichi by restricting critical mineral exports, a slowdown already hurting Japanese companies. The G7 agreed to create a critical minerals alliance with an expanded role for the IEA, aimed at reducing dependence on Chinese supply chains for rare earths and semiconductors.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is simultaneously fighting among its own members over digital taxes while trying to coordinate a response to China's trade dominance. Trump's willingness to tariff an ally's signature industry at 100% undermines any coherent G7 trade front. Meanwhile, China's weaponization of mineral exports to Japan shows how resource control is becoming the next frontier of economic statecraft.

sections.heading: DeepSeek Escapes US Blacklist as Trump Prioritizes China Trade Truce
sections.body:
The Trump administration has declined to add Chinese AI firm DeepSeek and memory chipmaker CXMT to the Commerce Department's Entity List, despite an interagency committee approving them — along with more than 100 other companies — for inclusion last year, Reuters and multiple outlets reported.

US officials had accused DeepSeek of supporting China's military and intelligence apparatus, and of using 'distillation' techniques to copy US AI models — allegations the company has denied. In April, the State Department instructed diplomats worldwide to raise concerns about Chinese AI model theft. But the Commerce Department has sat on the listings, which Reuters frames as part of a broader pause to avoid escalating US-China tensions during trade negotiations.

The decision illustrates the tension between national security hawks who want to choke off China's AI capabilities and trade negotiators who need a stable diplomatic environment. DeepSeek has become the litmus test: its low-cost models shocked the industry in January 2025, and blacklisting it would be the single most consequential AI-related export action to date. Instead, the administration is keeping the designation as leverage — available but not yet deployed.
sections.whyItMatters: The DeepSeek non-listing is a case study in how trade truces constrain security policy. Over 100 flagged companies remain in regulatory limbo, and the precedent — approve then shelve — weakens the credibility of the Entity List as a deterrent. For AI governance globally, it signals that Washington will trade off enforcement for diplomacy.

sections.heading: Central Banks Pull Gold Home as Reserve Strategy Shifts
sections.body:
The World Gold Council's annual survey, released at the G7, found that central banks bought 244 tonnes of gold in Q1 2026, maintaining an average purchase pace of roughly 1,000 tonnes per year over the past four years — double the rate of the previous decade. Nearly 89% of respondents expect global gold reserves to rise over the next 12 months, while 45% plan to increase their own holdings.

Perhaps more significantly, 9% of central banks increased domestic gold storage in the past year, up from 5% a year earlier, and another 10% diversified their overseas storage locations, up from 2%. The shift is directly linked to the freezing of roughly $300 billion in Russian foreign assets after the Ukraine invasion, which raised fears that gold held abroad — particularly at the Bank of England or the New York Fed — could become inaccessible during political disputes. UBS commodity analyst Giovanni Staunovo noted that France's central bank has been reducing US exposure by selling gold holdings in America and buying equivalent amounts in Europe.

UBS expects central banks to buy 750-1,000 tonnes of gold this year, providing a stable demand base even as jewelry and investment demand soften. The CNBC report noted that 7% of surveyed banks plan to increase domestic storage over the next year.
sections.whyItMatters: The repatriation trend is a slow-burn consequence of the Russia sanctions precedent: if reserves stored in New York or London can be frozen, they are not truly reserves. Every tonne moved home is a small vote of no confidence in the Western financial architecture — and a practical hedge against future sanctions coordination.

bottomLine: Central banks on three continents tightened policy this week — the BOJ affirming further hikes, the Fed's dot plot flipping to a hiking bias under new chair Warsh, and the ECB delivering its first rate increase since 2023 — all while inflation proves stickier than expected across developed markets. The oil market faces a whiplash: near-term inventory draws at a record pace even as the IEA projects a massive 2027 glut once Hormuz reopens. G7 unity fractured along trade lines, with Trump threatening 100% tariffs on French wine and China weaponizing mineral exports to Japan. And the deliberate US decision to withhold DeepSeek's blacklisting shows that trade diplomacy is now constraining security enforcement in AI. The connective thread: every major policy decision this week is being shaped by the tension between immediate crisis management (Iran war inflation, Asian currency defense) and the longer-term reordering of trade alliances, reserve strategies, and technology controls.
sources: AP News, Bloomberg, Reuters, CNBC, France24, Financial Express, BeInCrypto, Seeking Alpha, CryptoBriefing, World Gold Council, IEA, Kpler, Lloyd's List, Peterson Institute for International Economics, UBS, Deutsche Bank, PVM Oil Associates, The Diplomat, South China Morning Post, Yahoo Finance, MSN, Memeburn, The News (Pakistan), Global Times
